items.foco: Hemochromatosis: C282Y mutation of the HFE gene
items.title: C282Y mutation in HFE
items.contentHtml:
Hemochromatosis: C282Y mutation of the HFE gene

  • Hereditary hemochromatosis (HH) type 1 associated with C282Y mutation in the HFE gene (chromosome 6).
  • Homozygosity C282Y explains >80% of classic cases; Compound heterozygosity C282Y/H63D confers lower risk.
  • Incomplete penetrance: only 30-50% of homozygotes develop clinical iron overload.
  • Mechanism: loss of HFE function → decrease in hepcidin → increased intestinal iron absorption.
  • Trap examination: Isolated H63D does not cause typical HH; requires cofactor (alcohol, steatosis).

items.foco: Hemochromatosis: elevated ferritin and transferrin saturation
items.title: Ferritin and transferrin saturation
items.contentHtml:
Hemochromatosis: elevated ferritin and transferrin saturation

  • Transferrin saturation (TS) >45% on an empty stomach is the most sensitive and early screening marker.
  • Serum ferritin reflects iron deposits; values >300 ng/mL (men) or >200 ng/mL (women) suggest overload.
  • Elevated ferritin is not specific: inflammation, liver disease, neoplasia can elevate it (acute phase reactant).
  • Diagnostic confirmation: ST >45% + elevated ferritin → genetic study for C282Y.
  • Trap: normal ferritin does not rule out HH if ST is elevated; ST is earlier.

items.foco: Hemochromatosis: triad of hyperpigmentation, diabetes and cirrhosis
items.title: Classic HH Triad
items.contentHtml:
Hemochromatosis: triad of hyperpigmentation, diabetes and cirrhosis

  • Classic triad: cutaneous hyperpigmentation (tanning, due to melanin and iron deposition), diabetes mellitus (due to pancreatic damage) and cirrhosis of the liver.
  • Present only in advanced stages; Currently, it is diagnosed earlier by biochemical screening.
  • Hyperpigmentation predominates in photoexposed areas (face, back of hands) and mucous membranes.
  • Other manifestations: arthropathy (2nd-3rd MCF), cardiomyopathy, hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism.
  • Trap: the triad can also be seen in advanced alcoholic cirrhosis; differential with ST >45% and genetics.

items.foco: Hemochromatosis: treatment with phlebotomies and chelators
items.title: Treatment: phlebotomies and chelators
items.contentHtml:
Hemochromatosis: treatment with phlebotomies and chelators

  • First line: therapeutic phlebotomies (400-500 mL/session, 1-2 times/week) up to ferritin <50 ng/mL and ST <30%.
  • Maintenance phase: phlebotomies every 2-4 months to maintain ferritin between 50-100 ng/mL.
  • If contraindication (anemia, heart disease): oral chelators such as deferasirox (20-30 mg/kg/day) or subcutaneous deferoxamine.
  • Monitor: ferritin, ST, hemoglobin; avoid ferritin <20 ng/mL (risk of iron deficiency anemia).
  • Trap: vitamin C in high doses increases iron absorption and can precipitate arrhythmias; avoid it during phlebotomies.

items.foco: Hemochromatosis: risk of hepatocellular carcinoma and cardiomyopathy
items.title: Complications: hepatocellular carcinoma and cardiomyopathy
items.contentHtml:
Hemochromatosis: risk of hepatocellular carcinoma and cardiomyopathy

  • Hepatocarcinoma (HCC): 20-30% risk in cirrhosis due to HH, even after iron depletion; requires screening with ultrasound every 6 months.
  • Dilated cardiomyopathy due to iron deposition in the myocardium; causes heart failure and arrhythmias (ventricular tachyarrhythmias).
  • Others: diabetes mellitus, arthropathy (chondrocalcinosis), osteoporosis, hypogonadism.
  • Risk of HCC is greater if there is HCV coinfection or alcohol consumption.
  • Trap: HCC can appear even without cirrhosis in HH (recent studies), but screening is indicated if cirrhosis or fibrosis advanced.

items.foco: Wilson disease: ATP7B mutation on chromosome 13
items.title: ATP7B mutation in Wilson
items.contentHtml:
Wilson's disease: ATP7B mutation on chromosome 13

  • Autosomal recessive; ATP7B gene on chromosome 13 (13q14.3).
  • Function: encodes copper-transporting ATPase; mutation → failure of biliary excretion of copper → accumulation in liver, CNS, cornea.
  • More than 500 mutations described; in common Caucasian population H1069Q; in Asians R778L.
  • Almost complete penetrance if homozygosity; variable expression (hepatic vs neurological).
  • Trap: heterozygotes (carriers) may have a slight decrease in ceruloplasmin but do not develop disease.

items.foco: Wilson disease: Kayser-Fleischer ring in the cornea
items.title: Kayser-Fleischer ring
items.contentHtml:
less cases with neurological manifestations and ~50% with hepatic presentation.
  • It can be reversible with chelation treatment.
  • Do not confuse with Fleischer ring (keratoconus, iron deposition) or with senile arch.
  • Trap: in children with pure hepatic presentation it may be absent; does not rule out the diagnosis.

items.title: Low ceruloplasmin
items.contentHtml:
Wilson's disease: decreased serum ceruloplasmin

  • Serum ceruloplasmin <20 mg/dL (normal 20-40 mg/dL) is a diagnostic pillar.
  • Value <10 mg/dL highly suggestive; but it can be normal in up to 5-10% of patients (especially with inflammation).
  • False elevation due to estrogen (oral contraceptives, pregnancy) or inflammation (acute phase reactant).
  • Decrease also in Menkes disease, nephrotic syndrome, malnutrition, advanced liver failure.
  • Trap: normal ceruloplasmin does not exclude Wilson; free copper (not ceruloplasmin) >15 µg/dL and other tests are required.

items.foco: Wilson's disease: elevated copper in 24-hour urine
items.title: 24h urinary copper
items.contentHtml:
Wilson's disease: elevated copper in 24-hour urine

  • Copper in 24-hour urine >100 µg/day (normal <40 µg/day) is diagnostic criterion.
  • Proper collection: container free of copper; avoid contamination.
  • After D-penicillamine test (500 mg orally at the beginning and at 12 hours): excretion >1600 µg/day confirms diagnosis.
  • False positives: cholestasis, primary biliary cirrhosis, active hepatitis.
  • Trap: in asymptomatic children it can be <100 µg/day; High suspicion and genetic testing are required.

items.foco: Wilson's disease: neurological and hepatic symptoms
items.title: Neurological and hepatic symptoms
items.contentHtml:
Wilson's disease: neurological and hepatic symptoms

  • Hepatic: steatosis, chronic hepatitis, cirrhosis, fulminant liver failure (with negative Coombs hemolytic anemia and low alkaline phosphatase).
  • Neurological: intention tremor, dysarthria, dystonia, rigidity, drooling, abnormal gait; extrapyramidal and cerebellar symptoms.
  • Psychiatric: depression, psychosis, personality changes, impulsive behavior.
  • Age of presentation: earliest hepatic (children/adolescents), neurological in young adults (20-30 years).
  • Trap: acute hemolytic anemia + fulminant liver failure + low alkaline phosphatase = Wilson up to to prove the opposite.

items.foco: Wilson's disease: treatment with D-penicillamine and zinc
items.title: Treatment: D-penicillamine and zinc
items.contentHtml:
Wilson's disease: treatment with D-penicillamine and zinc

  • First line for symptomatic patients: D-penicillamine (750-1500 mg/day in 2-3 doses, 1 hour before or 2 hours after meals).
  • Adverse effects of D-penicillamine: induced lupus, myelosuppression, proteinuria, skin reactions; requires monitoring.
  • Alternative or maintenance: trientine (less toxic) or zinc (zinc acetate 50 mg 3 times/day) which blocks intestinal absorption of copper.
  • Zinc is the choice in asymptomatic patients, maintenance or pregnancy; fewer adverse effects.
  • Trap: add pyridoxine (vitamin B6) 25-50 mg/day with D-penicillamine to prevent deficiency neuropathy.

items.foco: Wilson's disease: avoid foods rich in copper
items.title: Diet: avoid copper
items.contentHtml:
Wilson's disease: avoid foods rich in copper

  • Restrict foods high in copper: liver, shellfish (oysters, crab), chocolate, nuts, mushrooms, legumes (chickpeas, lentils), cereals integral.
  • Drinking water: avoid well water or copper pipes; use filters if necessary.
  • Low copper diet complements pharmacological treatment; it does not replace it.
  • Strict restriction is not necessary if the patient is stable on chelators or zinc; avoid excesses.
  • Trap: avocado and tea have moderate content; not prohibited but limited.
